coredata.dc:title: Light-activated antibacterial electrospun polyacrylonitrile-graphene quantum dot nanofibrous membranes
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiz, Virginia
Maudes, Jon
Grande, Hans-Jürgen
Pérez-Marquez, Ana
coredata.dc:description: Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibrous membranes incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQD) as photoantibacterial additives were prepared by electrospinning. GQD encapsulation in the composite GQD-PAN membranes proved very stable, with negligible GQD leakage after 1 month immersed in neutral phosphate buffer solutions. A series of GQD-PAN membranes with different GQD content and electrospinning times were prepared to assess the influence of these parameters on the antibacterial activity of the membranes against Escherichia coli under visible light irradiation. Electrospinning time had a pronounced effect on membrane weight/thickness and nanofiber diameter, both parameters increasing with electrospinning time. Membranes with the optimal composition (1% wt. GQD and 1.5 h electrospinning time) showed excellent antibacterial activity against E. coli, with 99.9999% (6 logaritmic units) bacterial inactivation after 24 h illumination with visible light. Different bactericidal reactive oxygen species (ROS) were detected upon illumination of GQD solutions and GQD-PAN membranes, confirming photodynamic inactivation of the bacteria by GQD photosensitization. In terms of ROS production, GQD outperformed well-known photosensitizers crystal violet and toluidine blue O, both with long-established efficiency for photodynamic treatment of drug-resistant bacterial infections.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV-Vis transmittance (a) and PL emission (b) spectra of GQD-PAN membranes prepared with different electrospinning times: 1.5 h (GQD-PAN1), 3 h (GQD-PAN2) and 4.5 h (GQD-PAN3), a PAN membrane and aqueous GQD solution; (c) photographs of the membranes under visible and UV light (365 nm).
Characterization of GQD, PAN and GQD-PAN membranes manufactured with different electrospinning times: (a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRD, (c) TGA and (d) water contact angle measurements.

SEM images with different magnifications of GQD-PAN membranes prepared with different electrospinning times and corresponding nanofiber diameter distribution: (a-b-c) 1.5 h, (d-e-f) 3 h, (g-h-i) 4.5 h.

Factors affecting the light-driven antibacterial activity of GQD-PAN membranes against E. coli in terms of logarithmic viable E. coli numbers: (a) Effect of GQD loading in membranes prepared with 1.5 h electrospinning time and irradiated for 24 h; (b) effect of electrospinning time in membranes with 1% wt. GQD irradiated for 24 h; (c) effect of irradiation time in membranes with 1% wt. GQD prepared with 1.5 h electrospinning time.

(a) Changes in absorbance (decrease with time) and fluorescence (increase with time) at the characteristic wavelengths of ROS-sensitive colorimetric probes (RhB, NBT) and fluorimetric probes (DHR, DCFH-DA) upon illumination in presence of GQD. Corresponding absorbance/fluorescence changes in illuminated GQD-free solutions (light control) and in mixtures stored in darkness (dark control). (b) Fluorescence intensity of 10 μg mL−1 DCFH-DA in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) alone (control) and with different concentrations of GQD after 30 min in dark or illuminated. Inset: photographs under UV lamp of the irradiated samples, with GQD concentration increasing from left (control) to right.

(a) Fluorescence intensity (excitation: 495 nm) at different illumination times of 10 μg mL−1 DCFH-DA solutions in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) alone (control) and with 2 μg mL−1 of different photosensitizers: CV (purple), TB (blue) and GQD (pale brown). Inset: photographs of samples illuminated for 1 h taken under a UV lamp. (b) Fluorescence increase (F-F0) of 10 μg mL−1 DCFH-DA solution in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) after 2 h in contact with PAN and PAN-GQD membranes under dark and illuminated conditions. Control: Membrane-free DCFH-DA solution in 0.1 M PBS.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibrous membranes incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQD) as photoantibacterial additives were prepared by electrospinning. GQD encapsulation in the composite GQD-PAN membranes proved very stable, with negligible GQD leakage after 1 month immersed in neutral phosphate buffer solutions. A series of GQD-PAN membranes with different GQD content and electrospinning times were prepared to assess the influence of these parameters on the antibacterial activity of the membranes against Escherichia coli under visible light irradiation. Electrospinning time had a pronounced effect on membrane weight/thickness and nanofiber diameter, both parameters increasing with electrospinning time. Membranes with the optimal composition (1% wt. GQD and 1.5 h electrospinning time) showed excellent antibacterial activity against E. coli, with 99.9999% (6 logaritmic units) bacterial inactivation after 24 h illumination with visible light. Different bactericidal reactive oxygen species (ROS) were detected upon illumination of GQD solutions and GQD-PAN membranes, confirming photodynamic inactivation of the bacteria by GQD photosensitization. In terms of ROS production, GQD outperformed well-known photosensitizers crystal violet and toluidine blue O, both with long-established efficiency for photodynamic treatment of drug-resistant bacterial infections.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrospinning is a simple, low-cost and versatile method for fabricating continuous fibers with diameters ranging from micrometers to few nanometers. The importance of nanofibers stems from the fact that they possess a very large surface area to volume ratio, high porosity, adjustable pore size, high mechanical resistance, and versatility in functionality among others. These features make nanofibrous membranes manufactured by electrospinning ideal candidates for many applications under exponential growth in sectors such as biomedicine, pollutant removal, air filtration, membranes for batteries, composite reinforcement, sensing and smart textiles. For these applications most polymer nanofibers must be functionalized, either incorporating additives into the polymer solution during the electrospinning process or by post-electrospinning surface functionalization, including techniques such as coating, chemical vapor deposition (CVD) or core-shell electrospinning [1]. Thus, nanofiber functionalization by metal or metal oxide nanoparticles allowed development of sensing devices for monitoring environmental pollution [2], membranes for wastewater treatment [3] or membranes with antibacterial, antiviral and/or antifouling properties [4–6]. In the field of antibacterial electrospun nanofibers, other biocide additives have also been used such as antibiotics, quaternary ammonium compounds or biopolymers [7]. In this context, carbon quantum dots (CQD) and graphene quantum dots (GQD) have emerged as a promising new class of potent antimicrobial agents with photodynamic inactivation (PDI) capability of many types of microorganisms [8–14]. Activity of photoantimicrobials is based on light-triggered generation of highly reactive oxygen species (ROS) and redox species that are responsible of the antimicrobial action against many pathogens [15,16]. ROS such as singlet oxygen, superoxide anion and hydroxyl radicals have a broad activity spectrum and can attack simultaneously various biomolecular sites in the microbial targets (eg, proteins, lipids and nucleic acids). This non-specific damage makes it very unlikely that pathogens develop resistance, hence PDI represents a promising strategy to destroy antibiotic resistant microorganisms [17].

Additional benefits of carbon-based antibacterial nanomaterials include biocompatibility and lower cytotoxicity than metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, whose bactericidal mechanism is based on metal ion release [10]. More interestingly, CQD are easily degradable into CO2, CO and water under mild conditions [18] and can be produced inexpensively and sustainably from varied organic feedstock and even waste. All these clear benefits have triggered intense research on CQD and GQD as antimicrobial agents in the last few years, as highlighted by several recent reviews [8–14]. Thus, a plethora of GQD and CQD produced by different methods have been reported to exhibit remarkable antibacterial, antifungal and antiviral activity in vitro and in vivo, with very low minimal inhibitory concentration and broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity even against multidrug-resistant pathogens. Efforts have focused on relating the antimicrobial activity of different types of GQD and CQD to their surface chemistry [19,20], charge [21,22], curvature [23], heteroatom-dopant nature [24,25], precursor type/synthetic route [26,27] and the nature of the microorganism (specially antibiotic-resistant bacteria), together with understanding the PDI mechanism in-depth [15,26,28]. These studies investigate the antimicrobial activity of GQD/CQD in solution, typically co-dispersed with the pathogen or applied as formulations for topical treatment of bacterial infections [29]. In contrast, there are only few examples of practical applications of GQD-based antimicrobials incorporated in surfaces or bulk materials. These include Langmuir-Blodgett thin films of CQD on different substrates [30], polydimethylsiloxane or polyurethane-based composites coated with GQD [31–33], wound dressings impregnated with GQD [34–36] and membranes functionalized with GQD [37,38]. In these studies, incorporation of antimicrobial GQD in composites, textiles and membranes was carried out by impregnation or surface modification, implying that the antimicrobial agent can be easily released from the support material.

The problem of GQD release can be avoided by electrospinning, which is an attractive method to immobilize efficiently GQD as antimicrobial agents in nanofibrous membranes [39]. We have previously demonstrated very stable encapsulation of GQD as sensing probes in electrospun PAN nanofibrous filtering membranes without long-term GQD leaking from the membrane [40]. Here we report for the first time preparation of an electrospun GQD-containing PAN nanofibrous membrane with antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli under visible light irradiation. The influence of parameters such as GQD content, electrospinning time and light irradiation time on the antibacterial activity of the membranes has been studied. Membranes with the optimal composition showed excellent antibacterial activity against E. coli, with 99.9999% bacterial inactivation after 24 h illumination with visible light. Different ROS generated upon illumination of the GQD-PAN membranes were detected, supporting PDI of bacteria by encapsulated photoantimicrobial GQD.

Polyacrylonitrile (MW=150,000), pyrene (98%), ammonia (32%), hydrazine hydrate (60%), crystal violet (≥ 90%), dihydrorhodamine 123 (DHR, ≥ 95%), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT, ≥ 90%), 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA, ≥ 97%), NaCl (99%), KH2PO4 (≥ 99%), Na2HPO4 (≥ 99%), dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥ 99%)) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, ≥ 99%)) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Rhodamine B (RhB, ≥ 99%) and toluidine blue O (TB, ≥ 80%),) were purchased from Acros Organics. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water of Synthesis A10 from Millipore (18 Ω cm) (Massachusetts, USA). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

GQD were synthesized by hydrothermal condensation of pyrene, according to a method reported elsewhere [41]. Briefly, in a typical procedure (Fig. S1), pyrene (1 g) was nitrated into 1,3,6-trinitropyrene in 65% wt. HNO3 (80 mL) under refluxing and stirring for 16 h. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with deionized water and filtered through a 0.22 µm nylon membrane. Part of the resultant 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (1 g) was dispersed by ultrasonication in deionized water solution (0.1 L) containing 0.4 M ammonia and 1.5 M hydrazine hydrate for 1 h. The suspension was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200ºC for 8 h. After cooled to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm nylon membrane to remove insoluble carbon product, and further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 1 day to remove salt and small molecules. After this time, the pH of the washing solution decreased from the initial value of 11–12–7–7.5, indicating complete removal of impurities and by-products.

Membranes were fabricated using a made-home electrospinning unit, consisting of a high voltage (30 kV) power supply (Matsusada Precision Inc AU-30R1-L), a syringe pump, a grounded collector and a metallic needle connected to the high voltage power supply. The electrospinning set-up was operated within an environmental chamber where the humidity was adjusted by a dehumidifying air system. Dry GQD (0.5–1% wt. referred to the polymer weight) were dispersed in DMF and stirred vigorously for 1 h in the dark at room temperature. Then, 10% wt. PAN powder was added to the GQD-DMF solutions and were continuously stirred for 24 h under the same conditions. The electrospun GQD-PAN fibers were prepared by loading each solution in a 10 mL syringe fitted to a metallic needle with an inner diameter of 0.6 mm by a Teflon tube. Electrospinning solution was then dispensed at a flow rate of 0.6 mL h−1, at 15 kV voltage and the distance between needle and collector was 240 mm. A high distance was selected to ensure solvent evaporation (taking into account the high boiling point of DMF) and to obtain large sample areas suitable for all the characterization tests. All tests were carried out at room temperature (20–22 °C) and approximately 40% of relative humidity. The membranes were collected on an aluminum foil substrate and deposition times were 1.5 h, 3 h and 4.5 h for the GQD-PAN1, GQD-PAN2 and GQD-PAN3 membranes, respectively. Different deposition times were used to produce membranes with different thickness and evaluate the influence on the antibacterial activity.

The morphology of GQD-PAN electrospun nanofibrous membranes was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JSM 5910-LV JEOL) to examine nanofiber diameter, homogeneity and defect concentration. The average diameter and diameter distribution of GQD-PAN nanofibers was determined using an image analysis software (Image-J v1.38). Membrane thickness was measured with a Fischer Dualscope MP0RH-FP instrument. Absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured with a Jasco V-570 UV–VIS–NIR spectrophotometer and a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to identify the functional groups in GQD-PAN nanofibers. FTIR spectra were measured in a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrophotometer in the range 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. Membrane color was monitored with a Konica Minolta CM-2600D colorimeter. Color differences in CIELAB coordinates (L a b) between GQD-PAN and PAN membranes were estimated as

.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed with a TGA Q500 (TA instruments) at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under nitrogen atmosphere. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded between 10° and 60° (2θ) with 7 s/0.02° step size on a Bruker D8 Discover diffractometer with a Cu-Kα source (λ = 0.154 nm) and equipped with a LynxEye PSD detector. An Attension Theta Lite optical tensiometer was used to determine the water contact angle of the membranes. Water drops of 4.5 µL were placed on three different areas of the surface. The contact angles were recorded using the Laplace-Young fitting method.

In vitro degradation tests of electrospun membranes were carried out with 3 cm × 3 cm samples placed inside 20 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) for 28 days. Prior to the experiments, samples were dried for 2 h at 60 ºC and weighed to evaluate the mass loss by immersion in the simulated physiological fluid. After the test, the samples were washed several times with distilled water in order to remove salts from the simulated fluid.

The antibacterial activity of membranes against Escherichia coli CECT 516 was determined according to the ISO 22196:2011 test: Measurement of antibacterial activity on plastics and other non-porous surfaces. Triplicates were conducted for each type of membrane (PAN alone, GQD-PAN membranes with different GQD loadings and prepared with different electrospinning times) and test conditions (dark control and illuminated for different times). A broth of the bacteria strain Escherichia coli (CECT 516) was prepared at a concentration between 2.5 and 10 × 105 CFU mL−1. The concentration was verified by means of microbiological plate count analysis. Each test membrane was placed on a Petri dish and its surface was inoculated with the previously prepared bacterial broth. Subsequently, the inoculated surface of each membrane was covered with plastic film to exert a slight pressure on the bacterial inoculum. The covered test specimens were incubated at 37ºC and 100% humidity while illuminated by a fluorescent lamp (Osram Fluora L 18 W/77, ∼30 cm distance) for different times. Control tests were also carried out in dark conditions. Extractions were carried out after 1 h, 6 h, 15 h and 24 h of contact. For that, each membrane (with the plastic film) was placed into a sterile bag containing 50 mL of sterile phosphate saline buffer (pH 7.4) and then was shaken for 1 min into a stomacher. The generated extracts were collected in sterile tubes and determination of the viable bacteria was done by the pour plate culture method. The results were obtained as colony forming units (CFU) and logarithmic (log) value per cm2 of contact surface tested.

ROS generated upon illumination of GQD solutions or GQD-PAN membranes were investigated in presence of different ROS-sensitive probes. In all assays, vials containing the mixtures (GQD and the ROS-specific probe) or petri dishes with membranes immersed in ROS-probe solutions were irradiated with a compact fluorescent lamp (with 420–750 nm emission spectral range and 1000 lm luminous flux) at a 5 cm distance. Dark control tests were performed by keeping either mixtures or immersed membranes in dark. Light control tests were done by illuminating solutions of the ROS-specific probes in absence of GQD or membranes. Additionally, tests without the ROS-specific probes were carried out to assess a possible contribution of GQD or membrane photodegradation. In all tests, aliquots were taken from irradiated and dark samples at different time intervals to measure absorbance or fluorescence at the characteristic wavelengths of the ROS-sensitive probes. All tests and controls were conducted in triplicate. Typical mixture compositions and optical responses measured for each ROS-specific assay were as follows: (1) RhB as colorimetric ROS-sensitive probe: RhB (10 μg mL−1) and GQD (100 μg mL−1) were dispersed in deionized water and the mixtures were subjected to irradiation. The decrease of absorbance at 554 nm, characteristic of RhB, is indicative of dye degradation by photogenerated ROS (1O2, •OH). (2) NBT as colorimetric ROS-sensitive probe: NBT (10 μg mL−1) and GQD (1–10 μg mL−1) were dispersed in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) pH 7.4. The decrease of absorbance at 259 nm, characteristic of NBT, is indicative of oxidative attack by photogenerated O2 • -. (3) DHR for total ROS detection: DHR (2 μg mL−1) and GQD (1–5 μg mL−1) were dispersed in DMSO. An increase in green fluorescence (488 nm excitation, 530 nm detection) corresponds to oxidation of the redox-sensitive dye to rhodamine 123 by different photogenerated ROS (such as 1O2, O2 • - or •OH); (4) DCFH-DA for total ROS detection: DCFH-DA (10 μg mL−1) and GQD (1–10 μg mL−1) were dispersed in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). An increase in green fluorescence (495 nm excitation, 523 nm detection) is related to the highly fluorescent 2′, 7′ –dichlorofluorescein (DCF) compound resulting from oxidation of deacetylated DCFH by different photogenerated ROS (1O2, O2 • -, •OH).

GQD produced by hydrothermal condensation of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene have been extensively characterized in our previous studies [40,42], hence only the most relevant features are summarized here ( Table 1). GQD used as nanoadditives in PAN membranes have an average lateral size of 2.7 ± 0.4 nm and a thickness of 1.5 ± 0.4 nm. They contain abundant amine and hydroxyl groups originating from the precursor and media (hydrazine and ammonia) used in the condensation reaction. Thus, their chemical composition (in at%) estimated from XPS spectrum indicates 71.1% C, 18.2% O and 10.7% N contents. GQD aqueous solutions exhibit intense absorbance and luminescence bands in the UV-Vis region. The three well-defined absorption bands (located at 250, 290 and 400 nm), are assigned to the π-π * transition of the aromatic sp2 domains of GQD and electronic transitions between energy levels of surface states arising from the functional groups. GQD are also strong photoluminescence (PL) emitters, with an excitation-dependent PL emission. The most intense PL emission band, centered at 503 nm, is obtained with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. These characteristic optical fingerprints of GQD (data not shown) have been used to corroborate successful incorporation of the nanoadditives in electrospun GQD-PAN membranes produced from dispersions of GQD and PAN in DMF. Optical transmittance spectra of electrospun GQD-PAN membranes manufactured with three different electrospinning times (1.5 h for GQD-PAN1, 3 h for GQD-PAN2 and 4.5 h for GQD-PAN3) are compared in Fig. 1a, together with the spectra of a PAN membrane and a GQD aqueous solution. While PAN membranes are white, GQD-PAN membranes have the characteristic yellow-orange coloration of GQD (photographs in Fig. 1c). Thus, the transmittance spectra of GQD-PAN membranes resemble that of GQD solutions, with a clear decrease of transmittance at wavelengths < 550 nm. The overall optical transmittance of GQD-PAN membranes decreases with increasing electrospinning times, pointing at an increase in membrane thickness. Indeed, measured membrane thickness values were 2.6 ± 0.3 µm (GQD-PAN1), 4.9 ± 0.7 µm (GQD-PAN2) and 8.8 ± 0.9 µm (GQD-PAN3). Areal weight density of the different membranes, which was determined by weighing a 5 cm × 5 cm piece of each membrane, was 2.2 g m−2 for GQD-PAN1, 4.6 g m−2 for GQD-PAN2 and 5.6 g m−2 for GQD-PAN3 membrane. That is, thickness and areal weight density increase with electrospinning time but for long deposition times, when the nanofibrous membrane is thick enough, charges on the electrospinning jet accumulates on the collector resulting in less deposited fibers.

An increase of membrane coloration with electrospinning time was also detected with a colorimeter. The following color differences in CIELAB coordinates between the different GQD-PAN and PAN membranes (ΔE) were measured: 10.8 ± 0.3, 12.5 ± 0.2 and 14.0 ± 0.2 for GQD-PAN1, GQD-PAN2 and GQD-PAN 3, respectively. Incorporation of luminescent GQD in electrospun GQD-PAN membranes was also probed by fluorescence spectroscopy. Representative PL emission spectra of the three GQD-PAN membranes and water dispersed GQD at a same excitation wavelength (420 nm) are compared in Fig. 1b together with the spectrum of a GQD-free PAN electrospun membrane. Unlike PAN, GQD-PAN membranes exhibit a strong cyan emission under UV illumination (photographs in Fig. 1c), with a well-defined band centered at 478 nm in the PL spectra corresponding to emission from the GQD. However, PL emission of GQD in PAN membranes is blue-shifted with respect to that of water dispersed GQD, whose band is centered at 503 nm. This blue-shift, most likely caused by interactions between functional groups of GQD and the polymer host matrix, has been previously noted by us in PAN and other authors for GQD embedded in other matrices such as polystyrene and silica. It is worth indicating that very uniform emission intensity was measured across the whole membrane area (relative standard deviation < 10%), which is indicative of a homogeneous GQD distribution along the electrospun membranes. No significant differences in PL intensity were detected for GQD-PAN membranes manufactured with different electrospinning times, revealing that only the most external nanofiber layers seem to contribute to the measured emission in such thick membranes.

FTIR spectroscopy was used to characterize GQD, PAN and the three GQD-PAN membranes ( Fig. 2a). The spectra of PAN and GQD-PAN membranes display the characteristic bands of PAN corresponding to the stretching vibration of nitrile (at 2240 cm−1) and methylene groups (at 2930 cm−1) and the bending vibration of methylene (at 1450 cm−1). In addition, the presence of GQD can be resolved in the FTIR spectra of GQD-PAN membranes, specifically an enhanced absorption in the range of 3150–3400 cm−1 due to the abundant hydroxyl and amine edge groups of GQD and in the range of 1530–1660 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of aromatic CC bonds in GQD.

Membranes were also examined by XRD (Fig. 2b). XRD patterns of PAN membranes show the characteristic crystalline peak centered at 2θ angle of 16.8º, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane and an amorphous halo at 23.6º from the amorphous regions of the nanofibers. In turn, XRD spectrum of GQD shows the characteristic diffraction peak of graphite centered at 26.7º, corresponding to the (002) plane. This peak cannot be resolved in the diffractograms of GQD-PAN membranes due to the low GQD content (1% wt.). Therefore, XRD patterns of PAN and GQD-PAN membranes with similar nanofiber diameter distribution (GQD-PAN1) are very similar. However, the degree of crystallinity (relative intensity of the crystalline to amorphous peak) of GQD-PAN membranes increased with electrospinning time, which could be attributed to larger nanofiber diameter ( Fig. 3), as has been observed before in electrospun PAN mats with different average nanofiber diameters [43]. Likewise, incorporation of 1% wt. GQD did not have any effect in the thermal properties of GQD-PAN membranes, as indicated by the almost identical TGA curves of PAN and GQD-PAN1 membranes (Fig. 2c). TGA of PAN and GQD-PAN1 membranes exhibit an initial 4% weight loss due to moisture removal, followed by comparable onset temperatures for thermal decomposition of PAN fibers (at 256º) and a final combustion region (above 500º), with identical residual weight percentages (30%) at 900ºC.

Finally, wettability of PAN and GQD-PAN membranes was analyzed by water contact angle measurements. Neat PAN membranes are hydrophilic, with average static water contact angle of 28.5 ± 2.0°, as shown in Fig. 2d. Although PAN is hydrophobic in nature, highly hydrophilic PAN nanofiber mats have been previously prepared under comparable electrospinning conditions[44]. Water contact angle decreased in all GQD-PAN membranes to an average value of 13.0 ± 2.3°, due to the high hydrophilicity of GQD, increasing the hydrophilicity of GQD-PAN nanofiber membranes.

Fig. 3 shows SEM images of the GQD-PAN membranes produced with different electrospinning times and nanofiber diameter distribution calculated using Image-J analysis program. The membranes show a uniform and common randomly oriented nanofiber structure. No significant differences were noted between PAN and GQD-PAN nanofibers in high-magnification images (Fig. S2). Approximately 150 measurements were used to estimate the average nanofiber diameters and distribution (Fig. 3c, f and i) with Image-J image analysis program. Interestingly, average nanofiber diameter increased with electrospinning time from 444 ± 69 nm (GQD-PAN1) to 494 ± 58 nm (GQD-PAN2) and 676 ± 96 nm (GQD-PAN3). As indicated before, charge accumulation on the collector with electrospinning time provides less uniform electric field, causing a lower speed, bending stability and stretching of the jet, thus leading to nanofibers with larger diameters, especially in the GQD-PAN3 membrane.

Next, before assessing the antibacterial activity of the membranes, we examined the robustness of GQD immobilization in electrospun PAN nanofibers. This is crucial to determine that the antibacterial activity arises from membrane-bound GQD and not from GQD released into solution. PAN-GQD membranes were immersed for 1 month in 0.1 M PBS pH 7.4 (the same buffer used for antibacterial activity tests) and the UV-Vis absorbance and PL spectra of the soaking solutions were measured every week. The spectra of the leachates from the three GQD-PAN membranes after 1 month are shown in Fig. S3, together with those of a blank PBS and GQD solutions in PBS. As can be seen, both absorbance and PL intensity of PBS soaking solutions are comparable to those of a pristine control PBS and around 100-fold lower than that of GQD solutions with very low concentration (10–100 μg mL−1). The photographs (Fig. S3C) also illustrate a negligible leaking of GQD from GQD-PAN membranes after 1 month immersed in PBS, further corroborated by the unaltered membrane weight. The results demonstrate a very stable encapsulation of GQD in PAN nanofibers at this high loading (1% wt.), in agreement with previous findings for GQD-PAN membranes with lower GQD loading (0.5% wt.) [40].

The influence of three different parameters in the photo-activated antibacterial activity of GQD-PAN membranes against E. coli was explored, namely: GQD loading, electrospinning time and irradiation time. Fig. 4a compares the number of viable E. coli (in log10 CFU cm−2) after 24 h visible light illumination of GQD-PAN membranes with different GQD content (0.5% and 1.0% wt.). In control tests with GQD-free PAN membranes prepared with the same electrospinning time (1.5 h), the logarithmic number of viable E. coli after 24 h illumination was 6.1 CFU cm−2, whereas in comparable tests with GQD-PAN membranes the logarithmic number of viable bacteria were significantly reduced to 1.3 and 0.0 CFU cm−2 with 0.5% wt. and 1% wt. GQD loadings, respectively. That is, incorporation of 0.5–1% wt. GQD in PAN membranes results in approximately 5 log (≈ 99.999%) to 6 log unit (≈ 99.9999%) reductions of viable bacterial numbers in CFU cm−2. On the contrary, high bacterial viability was obtained in membrane-free 24 h illumination tests (6.0 log units in CFU cm−2) and in 24 h dark control tests with GQD-PAN membranes (5.4 log units in CFU cm−2). Thus, the remarkable antibacterial activity of illuminated GQD-PAN membranes demonstrates the PDI of E. coli by GQD embedded in PAN nanofibers. The antibacterial action of the membrane increases with GQD loading and can be attributed to membrane-bound photoantibacterial agents (non-freely diffusing) due to their stable immobilization in the nanofibers (see Section 3.1). This result is relevant as it differs from previous reports of antibacterial wound dressings impregnated with GQD, where they can be released and inactivate bacteria by contact killing (destabilizing bacterial membrane by electrostatic interaction). Here ROS photogenerated (see Section 3.3) by membrane-confined GQD seem to reach the pathogen and efficiently inactivate it.

Next, at the highest GQD loading (1% wt.), the antibacterial activity of GQD-PAN membranes manufactured with different electrospinning times (1.5–4.5 h) was explored to address the effect of nanofiber diameter and membrane thickness. Fig. 4b compares the number of viable E. coli after 24 h illumination of the three GQD-PAN membranes and the bacterial viability in membrane-free 24 h illumination tests (control test). Surprisingly, the membrane with the lowest electrospinning time (1.5 h) displayed the highest antibacterial activity, with almost complete E. coli inactivation after 24 h compared to the high bacterial viability (6.0 log in CFU cm−2) in the control test. The bactericidal function decreased in GQD-PAN membranes produced with longer electrospinning times, with the following logarithmic numbers of viable E. coli achieved: 3.7 and 5.5 log units in CFU cm−2 for GQD-PAN2 (3.0 h) and GQD-PAN3 (4.5 h) respectively. That is, for a same GQD loading, the antibacterial performance of the membranes is significantly reduced when thickness/weight and nanofiber diameter increases. The results seem to indicate lower surface accessibility of the photoantibacterial sites in thicker membranes composed of large-diameter nanofibers and hence lower amount of photogenerated ROS.

Finally, in order to illustrate the PDI mode of action, the antibacterial activity of the most active GQD-PAN membrane (with 1% wt. GQD and 1.5 h electrospinning time) was tested at different illumination times. As expected, bacterial viability decreases with illumination time (Fig. 4c), with the following logarithmic numbers of viable E. coli achieved for each irradiation time: 4.4 CFU cm−2 (1 h), 3.8 CFU cm−2 (6 h), 2.6 CFU cm−2 (15 h) and 0.0 CFU cm−2 (24 h). Thus, total inactivation was achieved after 24 h, in clear contrast with the high viable E. coli numbers (5.4 log units in CFU cm−2) obtained in control tests where the membranes were kept 24 h in dark.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of antibacterial action of GQD and GQD-PAN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate that PDI of bacteria is caused by ROS resulting from GQD photosensitization, a series of colorimetric and fluorimetric assays using different ROS-specific reporting probes were conducted. First, RhB dye (10 μg mL−1) was used as colorimetric reporter to detect 1O2 and •OH generated in GQD aqueous solutions (100 μg mL−1) upon visible light illumination. In presence of GQD, the characteristic absorbance band of RhB centered at 554 nm decreased with illumination time (Fig. S4A) due to RhB degradation by photogenerated ROS (1O2, •OH). However, in absence of light (dark control) or under illumination in GQD-free RhB solutions (light control), negligible changes in RhB absorbance were noted. The magnitude of absorbance changes after 6 h is compared in Fig. 5a for the three RhB tests: 1) illuminated RhB and GQD mixtures; 2) light control; and 3) dark control. Moreover, when illumination tests were carried out in presence of ROS scavengers such as D-mannitol (•OH) and L-tryptophan (1O2), RhB degradation was reduced by 20–30%, indicating that it is a ROS-mediated process.

A second colorimetric probe, NBT, was used for specific detection of photogenerated O2 • − radicals. The absorbance of NBT (10 μg mL−1) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) was measured both in presence and absence of GQD (1–10 μg mL−1) under illumination and dark conditions. In illuminated GQD-containing samples, a decrease of the characteristic absorption band of NBT at 259 nm with time was measured (Fig. S4B), which can be attributed to oxidative attack of NBT by photogenerated O2 • − radicals. In contrast, comparatively smaller absorbance changes occurred in dark and light (GQD-free) control tests with this O2 • − specific reporter (Fig. 5a), illustrating the need of both light and photosensitizer (GQD) for ROS production.

In order to provide further evidence of generation of the indicated ROS, two additional ROS-reporting fluorimetric probes were used, DHR and DCFH-DA, which are commonly used for detection of intracellular ROS levels [15,21] as well as oxidative stress in cell-free conditions [45] In the DHR assay, the redox-sensitive dye can be oxidized by various ROS (such as 1O2, O2 • - or •OH) to rhodamine 123, which emits bright green fluorescence. Thus, a clear increase in green fluorescence (488 nm excitation, emission band centered 530 nm) was measured after 2 h visible light illumination of DHR (2 μg mL−1) and GQD (1 μg mL−1) solutions in DMSO, as shown in Fig. S4C. On the other hand, 4-fold lower fluorescence enhancements and negligible fluorescence changes (Fig. 5a) were noted in GQD-free DHR solutions illuminated for the same time (light control) and in non-illuminated mixtures (dark control), respectively. Hence, this assay confirms generation of high ROS levels by photoexcited GQD. This was further supported by the DCFH-DA assay, where oxidation of deacetylated DCFH by different ROS yields a highly fluorescent green-emitting compound, 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). Fig. S4D shows the increase in green fluorescence (495 nm excitation, emission band centered at 523 nm) with illumination time (1 h) measured for DCFH-DA (10 μg mL−1) and GQD (2 μg mL−1) mixtures in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). An intense green emission was detected in illuminated GQD-containing samples, as opposed to the case of GQD-free samples or non-illuminated mixtures, where fluorescence hardly increased (Fig. 5a). In conclusion, assays with the four different ROS-sensitive probes indicate ROS generation by photosensitization of GQD.

In order to illustrate the photosensitizer role of GQD, the fluorescence intensity of DCFH-DA (10 μg mL−1) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) samples with different concentrations of GQD (0.5–3 μg mL−1) was measured after 30 min illumination or stored in darkness. In absence of GQD, very low fluorescence intensity values were measured both for illuminated and dark control samples (Fig. 5b). Conversely, fluorescence intensity increased with GQD concentration in illuminated samples while remaining unaltered in absence of light. The photograph in Fig. 5b shows the samples under a UV lamp (365 nm) after having been illuminated with visible light for 30 min, with GQD concentration increasing from left (no GQD) to right (3 μg mL−1). The results demonstrate that ROS production yield increases with GQD concentration. However, as indicated before, GQD exhibit cyan fluorescence and it could be argued whether their emission could contribute to the measured fluorescence from the oxidation product of DCFH-DA, DCF. To rule out the possible contribution of GQD emission in ROS-detection assays with DCFH-DA, the fluorescence spectra of GQD (3 μg mL−1) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) samples alone and with DCFH-DA (10 μg mL−1) were measured after 1 h illumination with visible light. As can be seen (Fig. S5), fluorescence emission of GQD at the excitation wavelength of DCF (495 nm) is negligible compared to the emission intensity of GQD and DCFH-DA mixtures. Therefore, it can be concluded that fluorescence in DCFH-DA assays with GQD can only be attributed to the fluorescent product from oxidation of DCFH-DA by photogenerated ROS upon GQD photoexcitation, DCF.

Next, the photosensitizer performance of GQD was benchmarked against well-known photosensitizers whose clinical use as photoantimicrobials has been widely assessed, namely crystal violet (CV) and toluidine blue O (TB) [46,47]. TB is a cationic phenothiazine dye (Fig. S6) that has proven to be effective for photodynamic treatment of drug-resistant bacterial infections while being safe to human tissues and cells [46]. In turn, CV is a triarylmethane dye (Fig. S6) that has been long used as topical antiseptic, treatment of infected wounds, with clinical tests showing their efficiency against methicillin-resistant bacteria [47]. The antibacterial action of both dyes has been associated with their photosensitizer activity and ROS generated upon photoexcitation. Here we conducted ROS detection assays with DCFH-DA as total ROS reporter in presence of a same concentration of the three different photosensitizers, CV, TB and GQD. The fluorescence intensity at 523 nm of DCFH-DA (10 μg mL−1) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) alone (light control) and with 2 μg mL−1 of the three photosensitizers is plotted in Fig. 6a at different illumination times. Overall, green fluorescence intensity increased with illumination time in samples with the three photosensitizers compared to the light control, revealing ROS production. Interestingly, GQD outperformed both photosensitizers in terms of ROS generation at all exposure times, with 4 and 12-fold higher fluorescence intensities than those measured for TB and CV, respectively. Photographs of the different samples taken under a UV lamp (365 nm) after 1 h illumination (inset, Fig. 6a) highlight the differences in green fluorescence intensity induced by the amount of ROS generated in each case. The remarkable photosensitizing capability of this type of GQD can be linked to its chemical composition, with a large π-conjugated system and abundant amine functional groups at edge sites (Fig. S6). Previous studies revealed that nitrogen-doping and amine moieties impart GQD with superior photosensitizer ability than their unmodified counterparts [25]. Moreover, a large π-conjugated system and strong electron donation in amino-functionalized GQD have been proposed to increase charge transfer efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers [25]. Besides this photosensitizer-promoting effect, a potent bactericidal action in charged (protonated) amine-functionalized CQD has been linked to their ability to disrupt the integrity of negatively-charged bacterial cell membrane by electrostatic charge interactions [19,20].

All these affects may explain the remarkable antibacterial activity against E. coli noted for the electrospun GQD-PAN membranes with nanofiber-bound GQD. In this regard, we finally explored the photosensitizing activity of GQD-PAN membranes using DCFH-DA as fluorescence reporter of ROS photogenerated upon membrane illumination. Three pieces (2 cm × 2 cm) cut out of each type of membrane (PAN, GQD-PAN1, GQD-PAN2 and GQD-PAN3) were placed in individual petri dishes and irradiated with visible light while being immersed in 10 μg mL−1 DCFH-DA solutions in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). Immersed membranes were also stored in darkness (dark control tests) and the DCFH-DA solution without a membrane was illuminated under the same conditions (light control tests). Additionally, membranes immersed in 0.1 M PBS (without DCFH-DA) were also illuminated to assess membrane photodegradation. In all tests, aliquots were taken from the different soaking solutions at various time intervals to measure fluorescence at 523 nm (excitation wavelength: 495 nm). The increase in fluorescence after 2 h (ΔF = F-F0) for the different soaking solutions in contact with PAN and the three GQD-PAN membranes under dark and illuminated conditions are compared in Fig. 6b. As can be seen, fluorescence enhancements in solutions bathing illuminated GQD-PAN membranes were 2.5–3-fold higher than those in solutions bathing illuminated PAN membranes and a membrane-free DCFH-DA solution (light control). This proves ROS generation by illuminated GQD-PAN membranes in the DCFH-DA/PBS assay solutions and production of the oxidation product with green fluorescence. In contrast, a negligible increase of fluorescence was measured for all samples stored in darkness, supporting the photosensitization mechanism. Likewise, the null fluorescence enhancement noted in illuminated GQD-PAN membranes immersed in PBS without the DCFH-DA probe rules out other possible contributions, like from GQD leaking from the membranes or compounds from membrane photodegradation.

We have demonstrated that encapsulation of GQD in PAN nanofibrous membranes produced by electrospinning represents an attractive approach for engineering photo-antibacterial membranes. Varying the electrospinning process parameters and membrane composition allows tuning nanofiber diameter, membrane thickness and loading of the biocide active agent, which in turn has a pronounced effect in the activity of the GQD-PAN membranes against E. coli. Thus, at the optimal composition membranes exhibited remarkable activity against E. coli, allowing 99.9999% (6 logaritmic units) bacterial inactivation after 24 h illumination with visible light. Tests with ROS-specific probes allowed detection of various bactericidal ROS upon illumination of GQD-PAN membranes due to GQD photosensitization, corroborating a mechanism based on photodynamic inactivation of the bacteria. Moreover, benchmarking tests revealed that GQD used as photoantimicrobial additives outperformed in terms of ROS production photosensitizers with well-known efficiency for photodynamic treatment of drug-resistant bacterial infections. Hence, these results could pave the way for the development of antibacterial surfaces and materials based on photoantimicrobial carbon nanoadditives.

Virginia Ruiz: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing- Original draft preparation. Jon Maudes: Investigation, Writing- Original draft preparation. Hans-Jürgen Grande: Writing- Reviewing. Ana Pérez-Marquez: Investigation, Writing- Original draft preparation.
